checks.finding: Round-1's two AMBIGUITY items are resolved with consistent, single-source phrasing. Human-copy timing ('applies the staged candidate as a commit on the feature PR branch BEFORE merge... until that commit exists the PR's CI stays red -- the designed fail-closed gate, with no staged-candidate fallback') is repeated identically in requirements.md Overview, REQ-002, AC-011, Roles and Permissions, Main Workflows T-003, and Edge Cases with no divergent wording. The dedup marker is now the literal `[model-freshness-divergence]` (Field Definitions), cited verbatim and identically from REQ-001/AC-002 (D1 manual path) and REQ-002/AC-007 (D2 automated path), and locked as document-conformance by acceptance-tests.md TEST-002.

No direct conflicts found. Human-copy timing statements across Overview, REQ-002, AC-011, Roles and Permissions, Main Workflows T-003, and Edge Cases agree exactly. AC-006's fail-soft language and the Edge Cases' explicit fail-soft-vs-fail-closed distinction do not conflict with REQ-002's no-bypass-on-genuine-drift constraint, which the spec itself frames as 'the mirror image' of the opposite failure mode rather than an overlapping rule.

requirements.md AC-006 requires fail-soft behavior 'when either the Anthropic or the OpenAI fetch fails' (REQ-002: 'on ANY fetch failure'), but acceptance-tests.md TEST-006 scopes its fixture only to 'both vendor sources' failing together. No AC/TEST fixture exercises the asymmetric case (one vendor succeeds, the other fails), which per AC-006's own wording must still take the fail-soft path rather than diffing on the successful vendor's partial data alone. This is REQ-002's explicitly-designated central, non-negotiable edge case (Edge Cases: 'the one place in this spec where fail-soft... is the correct and required behavior'), so a test author following acceptance-tests.md's literal fixture description would not build the asymmetric-failure scenario, and an implementation that only fail-softs on total dual-vendor failure would violate AC-006 with no test to catch it. This belongs to spec review because it is a Phase 1 acceptance-test completeness gap for a state the requirement text itself creates, not an implementation-detail question; Major matches the calibration bar for a high-risk, non-negotiable behavior with an incomplete validation surface.

requirements.md Assumptions section's four items (C1 schema shape, self-improvement-pr-guard.sh denylist scope, guard_invariants.py protected_gate_suffixes, agent-model-routing.tests.sh assert_literal matching) each carry an explicit contingency ('if C1's landed schema differs, T-002/T-003 re-verify against the landed file before implementing,' 'if a future edit removes .github/workflows/test.yml from that list... the carve-out becomes unnecessary but harmless to keep'), leaving nothing unresolved for design/task decomposition.

The human-copy protected-file boundary (AC-011) is now concretely testable: TEST-011 verifies the staged candidate + MANIFEST.sha256 and confirms the live file is unmodified at staging time, and TEST-009's live-file self-check is explicitly designed to stay red until the human's pre-merge commit lands on the feature PR branch, with no staged-candidate fallback -- CI itself is the observable proof mechanism for the human's approval action, resolving round-1's timing ambiguity that previously blocked testability.

The two previously-inventable behaviors from round 1 (human-copy timing, dedup marker sharing) are now explicit, single-source spec decisions requiring no downstream invention. The newly-identified AC-006/TEST-006 asymmetric-fetch-failure gap does not force a design reviewer to invent product behavior -- AC-006's 'either' wording already unambiguously defines the required behavior; only test-scenario coverage is incomplete, which is captured independently under EDGE-CASE-COVERAGE rather than requiring the design gate to author new behavior.
